junior.question: What parameter controls the initialization scaling factor in a LoRA configuration?
junior.core_intent: Checks understanding of LoRA config parameters.
junior.explanation: The scaling factor is controlled by `lora_alpha`. The adapter weights are scaled by `lora_alpha / r`. A higher alpha increases the influence of the adapter weights relative to the base model weights.
junior.star_framework_tip: Explain the relationship between rank and scaling alpha.
common_pitfalls: Believing that increasing rank automatically scales up adapter activations.

junior.question: What does the rank r control in a LoRA configuration?
junior.preparation_guide: Recall r as the low-rank update's capacity.
junior.explanation: r sets the rank of the low-rank update matrices, determining the adapter's capacity and how many trainable parameters it adds; higher r means more capacity (and more parameters and overfitting risk), lower r means a cheaper, more regularized adapter.
junior.star_framework_tip: State r as the capacity/parameter dial.
common_pitfalls: Confusing r with the alpha scaling factor.

references.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of LLMs (Hu et al.)

junior.question: Why fine-tune a pretrained model instead of training from scratch?
junior.preparation_guide: Recall data/compute savings from reused knowledge.
junior.explanation: Fine-tuning reuses the language, knowledge, and representations learned during pretraining, so it adapts to a task with far less data and compute and avoids relearning language from scratch.
junior.star_framework_tip: Say it reuses pretrained knowledge to save data and compute.
common_pitfalls: Thinking from-scratch training is feasible for typical task budgets.

references.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of LLMs (Hu et al.)

junior.question: In instruction fine-tuning, why are prompt tokens masked from the loss?
junior.explanation: Masking the prompt makes the loss apply only to the assistant response, so the model learns to generate good answers rather than to predict the user's words, improving instruction following.
junior.star_framework_tip: State response-only loss as the reason.
common_pitfalls: Training on the whole sequence including the prompt.

mid_level.question: Why is it recommended to target all linear layers (e.g. q_proj, v_proj, gate_proj, up_proj) rather than just attention projections during LoRA fine-tuning?
mid_level.preparation_guide: Compare fine-tuning capabilities when adapting FFN and projection weights.
mid_level.explanation: Targeting only attention projections (like query/value) can limit model adaptibility. Research shows that targeting all linear layers (including FFN projection matrices) improves performance on specialized tasks, matching full fine-tuning performance.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: Cite findings from PEFT scaling studies.
common_pitfalls: Claiming targeting more layers decreases computational throughput (it has minimal impact on latency).

mid_level.question: How does the LoRA alpha interact with r, and what is the effect of scaling alpha?
mid_level.explanation: The adapter update is scaled by alpha/r, so alpha controls how strongly the low-rank update influences the base relative to its rank. Raising alpha increases the update's effect; because of the division by r, alpha and r must be tuned together to keep the effective scale sensible.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: Explain the alpha/r scaling and joint tuning.
common_pitfalls: Treating alpha as an absolute scale independent of r.

references.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of LLMs (Hu et al.)

mid_level.question: When would you choose QLoRA over LoRA over full fine-tuning?
mid_level.preparation_guide: Map each to its resource/quality regime.
mid_level.explanation: Use full fine-tuning when you need maximum quality and have ample memory and per-task storage. Use LoRA when you want most of the quality with far less memory and tiny per-task checkpoints. Use QLoRA when even the base does not fit, since 4-bit loading plus adapters enables fine-tuning large models on a single consumer GPU, accepting a small quality cost.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: Decision rule by memory budget: full FT -> LoRA -> QLoRA as memory tightens.
common_pitfalls: Reaching for full fine-tuning when LoRA/QLoRA would meet the need cheaply.

references.title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of LLMs (Hu et al.)

mid_level.question: How does instruction tuning relate to and precede preference optimization?
mid_level.preparation_guide: Explain SFT as the base for DPO/RLHF.
mid_level.explanation: Instruction tuning (SFT) teaches the model to follow instructions by imitating demonstrations, producing a competent assistant. Preference optimization (DPO or RLHF) then refines that model using comparative feedback to prefer better, safer responses. SFT comes first because preference methods work best when starting from a model that already follows instructions.
common_pitfalls: Applying DPO/RLHF to a base model that has not been instruction-tuned.

references.title: Training LMs to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback (InstructGPT)

senior.question: Explain how to merge LoRA adapter weights back into a base model's weight tensors to eliminate inference latency overhead.
senior.core_intent: Tests knowledge of post-training integration and deployment pipelines.
senior.explanation: Consolidate the adapter weights using: $W_{final} = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r}(B \times A)$. In Hugging Face, run `model = model.merge_and_unload()`. This fuses the adapter weights back into the base model parameters, eliminating the computational overhead of running separate adapter passes.
senior.star_framework_tip: Detail VRAM calculations for running separate adapters vs. merged models.
common_pitfalls: Attempting to merge models trained with different base vocabulary architectures.

senior.question: Design a cost-effective way to serve dozens of task-specific LoRA adapters on shared infrastructure.
senior.preparation_guide: Cover shared base, swappable adapters, and batching.
senior.explanation: Keep a single base model resident in GPU memory and load small adapters on demand, hot-swapping or even batching requests across different adapters with a serving stack that supports it. Maintain an adapter registry with versioning and a base-model fallback. This amortizes the costly base across many tenants and makes adding a task a few-megabyte deployment instead of a full model.
senior.star_framework_tip: Center on one resident base plus swappable, versioned adapters with multi-adapter batching.
common_pitfalls: Serving a separate merged full model per task and exhausting memory.

senior.question: After domain fine-tuning, your SLM has lost general ability. Propose a remediation plan.
senior.preparation_guide: Combine data, method, and evaluation fixes.
senior.explanation: Re-run fine-tuning with a lower learning rate and fewer epochs, mix general-purpose data back in (rehearsal), and switch to LoRA so the frozen base preserves general knowledge; if a merged model already forgot, retrain from the base rather than continuing. Add held-out general benchmarks to the eval gate so forgetting is caught automatically next time, and keep adapter versions for rollback.
senior.star_framework_tip: Combine smaller updates + data replay + PEFT, and add general-eval gating.
common_pitfalls: Continuing to fine-tune the already-degraded model instead of restarting from the base.
